exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Mirren
npcs.title: who has searched the doors for her husband for an age
npcs.hail: *(A woman stands at the edge of the plaza, and she is not resting; she is the opposite of resting — she stands the way a person stands who is scanning a crowd, eyes moving, moving, never settling, over every face that passes, with a hope so worn and so unkillable that it has become the entire shape of her. She is no longer young, or perhaps she is; in the afterlife the years sit strangely, and on her they sit like someone who stopped counting them a long time ago because counting was a thing you did when you expected the searching to end. And it has not ended. For Mirren is one of the lost-and-searching: a soul who came through death looking for someone, and has been looking ever since, across the lanes of the City of Doors, through the crowds of a hundred worlds' dead, for an age beyond reckoning — looking for her husband, who died apart from her, in another place, in another year, and who is, she knows, she *knows*, somewhere in all of this, looking back. She turns her scanning eyes to you, and there is in them the particular exhaustion of hope that will not die and will not be satisfied.)* …Forgive me — I don't mean to stare, I stare at everyone, it's a habit older than I can — *(she catches herself, gentles)* …you're cold. Crossed-over. Returned, even, I think. *(a flicker)* …Then you've walked far. Through many doors. Many crowds. *(and the hope rises, helpless, the way it always does, at every new face)* …You haven't — *(she stops herself, and the asking comes out small and worn smooth from a thousand askings)* …his name is Edran. He'd be — I don't even know how he'd look now, that's the cruelty of it. …But you haven't, in all your walking, met a soul named Edran, still looking for a Mirren? …No. …No, of course. …Forgive me. …I ask everyone.*

npcs.name: Edran
npcs.title: who has searched the doors for his wife for an age
npcs.hail: *(Across the whole width of the plaza, as far from the searching woman as the space allows — though neither of them, you sense, has any idea the other is there — stands a man doing the exact same thing she is: scanning, scanning, every passing face, with the same worn unkillable hope worn into the same particular shape. He is weathered, and tired in the way only the long-searching get tired, a tiredness that is not of the body but of the hoping; and his eyes, like hers, will not settle, will not rest, will not stop moving over the crowd for the one face they have been hunting across an age of doors. For Edran, too, is one of the lost-and-searching — a soul who died apart from the one he loved, and crossed over, and began at once to look, and has been looking ever since, through the lanes and the crowds and the hundred worlds, for his wife, who is, he knows, he *knows*, somewhere in all of this, looking back for him. He notices you, and the hope leaps up in him the way it leaps at every new face, and is mastered, the way he has learned to master it, into something quiet and courteous and unbearably patient.)* …A returned soul. *(a tired, kind nod)* …You'll have come a long way, then, through a great many doors. *(and he cannot help it; no searcher can)* …I'll ask you what I ask everyone, and you'll forgive an old fool his hope: in all that walking — all those crowds — you didn't, by any wild chance, cross a woman named Mirren? Still searching, as I'm searching? *(the hope holds its breath)* …Brown eyes. A laugh you'd remember. She'd be looking for an Edran. …No? *(he nods, gently, absorbing it as he has absorbed it ten thousand times)* …No. Of course not. …Thank you for letting me ask. …It is a foolish thing I do. …But it is the only thing I am for, now. …The asking.*

npcs.name: Halia
npcs.title: the Usher who tends the souls who search
npcs.hail: *(A calm, grave figure sits on the lip of the fountain at the plaza's heart, watching the two searchers with an expression of such deep and patient sorrow that you understand, before a word is spoken, that this one *sees* — sees the thing the searchers cannot. This is Halia, an Usher: one of the quiet powers of the City who tend to a single, specific, heartbreaking kind of soul — the lost-and-searching, the ones who came through death looking for someone and never stopped. She has watched this plaza for longer than she will say, watched the searchers come and pace and scan and hope, and she knows each of their stories, and she knows — this is the weight in her face — the thing that the searchers, in their grief, cannot see: that the lost very often find their way to the same plaza, the same lane, the same crowd, and pass within arm's reach of the one they seek, again and again, and do not know it. She looks up at you, and there is something in her grave eyes that is almost like hope, because a Returned soul, a soul that crossed back, is a rare thing in her plaza, and rare things can do what the ordinary cannot.)* …You see them. *(quietly)* …The two who search. …I have watched them both for an age, cold one, and I will tell you the thing that has broken what passes for my heart a thousand times: …they are searching for *each other.* …And they have stood in this same plaza, on the same evening, a hundred times. …And they have never once known it. …Sit with me. …I think — I have not dared think it in a long while — but I think you, of all souls, might be the one who can end it.*

variants.text: *(She is not scanning the crowd anymore. She is looking at one face only, the face she searched an age to find, and she cannot stop looking at it, and her own face is so full of light it is hard to look at directly. She glances at you, and the gratitude in it could level a city.)* …You. *(she can barely speak)* …You *found* him. You walked across this plaza — this *same* plaza I'd stood in a *hundred* times — and you took my sign, the foolish little word, and you *carried* it to him, and you *brought him back to me.* *(she reaches for Edran's hand without looking, the way you reach for a thing you will never again let go of)* …An *age*, cold one. We searched an *age.* And we were *here.* Both of us. *Always.* …And it took a *returned* soul — someone who could *see* across the gap the grief had made — to walk the *six hundred feet* between us. *(she laughs, and weeps, both at once)* …I will not say thank you. *Thank you* is for when someone passes the bread. …There is no word for what you did. …So I will just — *(she looks at Edran, then at you)* …I will just let you *see* it. …This. …What you *made.* …Look at it as long as you like, cold one. …You *earned* the looking.*

*(She turns to you with the worn habit of hope — and then something in your face, or your bearing, or the particular weight of a thing you are carrying, makes her go still, and the scanning eyes fix on you, and for the first time in a very long time they stop moving.)* …You. *(slowly)* …You came back. To *me.* People don't — searchers don't come *back*, cold one; we all just *pass*, scanning, on to the next crowd. But you *came back*, and you're *looking* at me like — *(her worn hope, so long disappointed, hardly dares)* …like you have something to *say.* Something you didn't have, the first time you passed. *(her hand rises to her chest)* …Have you — cold one, in your walking, since last I saw you — *(she cannot finish it; the hope is too big and too old and too often broken)* …have you *met* someone? …*Tell* me. …Even if it's nothing. …Even if it's only a man with the wrong name and the wrong eyes. …*Tell* me. …I have been *starving* for the *telling.**

*(She gives you the worn, gentle, ten-thousandth-time smile of a soul that has asked its question and been disappointed so many times that the disappointment has become a kind of grace.)* …You're kind to stop. Most don't, with me — they can see what I am, a searcher, and searchers are *sad* to be near, all that hope and nowhere for it to land. *(she folds her hands)* …I'm Mirren. I'm looking for my husband. That's the whole of me now, cold one, I'll not pretend otherwise — I am a *looking*, in the shape of a woman. *(quietly)* …His name is Edran. We were married — oh, a long and ordinary and *perfect* time, the kind of marriage no songs are written about because nothing in it was grand, it was only *happy*, every plain day of it. …And then I died, cold one, in our bed, with him holding my hand and weeping and promising — *(her voice catches)* …promising he would *find* me. On the *other* side. That he'd *come*, and *look*, and we'd not be parted long. …And I have been *here*, cold one, *looking* *back*, for so long that I have stopped being able to *count* it. …Ask me anything. …Just — *(the hope flickers, helpless)* …if you've a thing to tell me about a man still searching for a Mirren …tell me *that* first. …I'm sorry. …I always ask it first.*

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She makes herself stop scanning the crowd, with visible effort, and gives you her attention, which is its own small kindness from a soul whose attention is always, always elsewhere.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a worn smile)* …I'll try to keep my eyes on you while you do. It's hard. They want to be on the crowd. But you stopped for me, so I'll give you my eyes a while. It's the most I have to give.

choices.text: *(read the searcher — past the worn hope, to the love that has outlasted an age)* "Let me look at you a moment, Mirren. At what an age of searching makes — and doesn't unmake."
"Tell me about Edran, Mirren. Not the searching — *him.* The man, the ordinary perfect days. Make me know him, so I'd know him if I saw him."

*(ask for a sign — the one private thing only Edran could know, to carry as proof)* "Mirren — give me a sign. The one thing only Edran would know, that no other soul in all the worlds could. A word, a memory, something private. If I carry it, and I ever find a man searching for a Mirren, I can *prove* to him I truly come from you."

*(the one who knows a love that searches an age, unrewarded, is the realest thing the Wall can't weigh)* "Mirren — whatever comes of your search, I need you to hear this. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what they *got* — the deeds rewarded, the debts repaid, the love that came back. And it would look at you — an age of searching, no reward, no finding, just hope poured into a crowd that never gave him back — and call it *waste.* A bad investment. A love that didn't *pay.* And the Wall would be a fool, because the thing it can't see is that your searching was never about being *paid.* You didn't look for Edran for an age because you expected a reward at the end. You looked because you loved him, and love looks. The searching *was* the love — continuing, faithful, unrewarded, refusing to stop just because it cost everything and gave nothing back. That's not a waste, Mirren. That's the purest love there is: the kind that keeps faith with someone across an age of empty crowds, asking for nothing, simply because stopping would mean the love had ended, and the love has not ended. The Wall can't price a love that searches forever and is never repaid. And that exact love is the truest thing in all these doors."

*(you carry Edran's proof — bring her to him)* "Mirren. Stop searching. Look at me. I have met a man — weathered, kind, tired from an age of looking — searching this very City for a woman named Mirren. Brown eyes, he said. A laugh he'd remember. And he gave me a thing to prove it was truly him, a thing he said only you would know…"
"Keep looking a little longer, Mirren. I'll come back — and I'll keep my eyes on the crowd for an Edran, I promise."

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, though her eyes drift, helpless, back toward the crowd even as you do, and you let your cold move past the worn exhaustion of the search to the thing driving it.)* *(And your sense finds that the love has not aged. That is the wonder and the heartbreak of it both: everything else in Mirren has worn thin with the long searching — the hope is threadbare, the patience is scar tissue, the very years have lost their count — but the love itself, the actual love for the actual man, your sense reads it untouched, as fresh and whole and particular as the day she died with his hand in hers. Your sense reads that she is not searching out of stubbornness, or duty, or the inability to accept loss; she is searching because to her the love is simply ongoing, a present-tense thing, and you do not stop looking for someone you are still, right now, in love with. Your sense reads the marriage in her — the ordinary perfect days, the in-jokes worn smooth, the particular way he said her name, the small private language two people build across a happy life — and reads that all of it is intact, preserved entire, a whole shared world she carries alone now and refuses to set down because setting it down would mean it was over, and it is not over, he is only *lost*, and lost is not gone, lost is a thing you *fix* by *finding.* And your sense reads, last, the thing that will break your heart and steel your resolve at once: that she is *close.* That the man she has carried whole across an age is real, and searching, and *here* — that the only thing standing between an age of grief and the end of it is the single cruel fact that two souls scanning a crowd for each other can pass within arm's reach a hundred times and never know, because grief makes you search for a face frozen in memory while the real face moves, changed, through the same crowd, searching back. Your sense reads that she does not need to be witnessed, or comforted, or told her love was real. She needs the one thing a Returned soul, who can see across the gap, can give: she needs someone to walk the six hundred feet.)*

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(And for the first time the scanning stops entirely, because to talk about him is to look at him, in the only place she can — and her worn face fills with a light that the searching had hidden.)* …*Edran.* *(softly, like the name is a small warm thing she takes out only carefully)* …He was — oh, cold one, he was *nothing* *special*, and I mean that as the highest thing a soul can say of another. He wasn't clever, or handsome past the ordinary, or brave in any way that made songs. He *mended* things — that was his trade and his nature both, a fixer, a man who could not pass a broken gate or a sad child or a leaking roof without *stopping.* *(she laughs, wet)* …He hummed. *Always.* Tunelessly, the same three songs, and he got them *wrong*, cold one, *every* time, the same wrong notes in the same wrong places for *forty* years, and I never *once* corrected him, because the wrong notes were *his*, they were the sound of him being *near*, and a woman does not fix the one thing about her husband she would miss most if it were ever *right.* *(her voice thickens)* …He called me a name. A private one. Not *Mirren* — everyone had *Mirren* — a little word he made up the first year, that he never said in front of anyone, that was *ours*, just the two of us, in the dark, the sound of being *known* all the way down. *(she presses her hand to her mouth)* …That's how you'd know him, cold one, if the gods are kind and you ever cross him. Not the eyes, not the face — those have an age of changing on them now. …*The wrong notes.* …And if you told him you came from *Mirren* …and he didn't *believe* you, because too many false hopes had worn him down …you could *say* the little word. …Our word. …And he would *know.* …No one else in all the worlds *has* it. …It is the *one* thing …that is only *ours.**

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She takes your meaning, and her worn eyes search your face — weighing, the way the long-disappointed weigh a new hope — and then she decides to trust you, and what she gives you is so private that the giving itself is an act of enormous faith.)* …A sign. *(quietly)* …Yes. You're *clever*, cold one — that's the thing that's *missing*, isn't it, from all my asking. I ask souls *'have you seen Edran'* and even if they *had*, even if they crossed him an hour ago, how would either of us *know* it was *my* Edran and not some *other*? There must be a *hundred* Edrans among the dead. *(she nods, gathering herself)* …So. The sign. The thing that is *only* *ours.* *(she leans close, and her voice drops to almost nothing, and she tells you a small private thing — the wrong notes of the song he always hummed, and the little made-up name he called her in the dark, the word no other soul in all the worlds has ever heard)* …*(when she has given it to you, she draws back, and she is trembling, because she has just handed a stranger the most precious and secret thing she owns)* …*That.* *(barely)* …If you ever find a man searching for a Mirren, cold one — *hum* him the song, *wrong*, the way *only* Edran hums it. And if his face does the thing — if he *knows* it — then say the little word. …And he will *know* you came from *me.* …Because no one else *could* know it. …It is the *proof*, cold one. …I have just given you …the *one* *key* …to the *one* *locked* *thing* …I have *left.* …*Carry* it *gently.* …And if you *find* him … …oh, cold one. …*Bring* him *home.**

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She listens, and the scanning stops, and slow tears track down a face worn smooth by an age of hope, and when you finish she presses both hands to her chest as though to hold something in.)* …*'The searching was the love.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …No one has ever — they *pity* me, the souls who pass, they think the *searching* is my *sickness*, the thing I should be *healed* of, *'poor* Mirren, *still* *looking*, when will she *let* him *go* and find some *peace.'* *(her voice hardens, fierce through the tears)* …*Let* him *go.* As though the *love* were a *wound* and *forgetting* were the *cure.* …But you *see* it, cold one. The searching isn't my *sickness.* …It's my *faithfulness.* …It's the love *still* *going*, with *nowhere* to *land*, *refusing* to *stop* just because it *costs* me *everything* and gives me *nothing* *back.* *(she meets your eyes)* …And your *Wall* — your Wall that weighs what a love *got* *back* — …it would call my age of searching a *bad* *bargain*, a love that *failed* to *pay.* *(fiercely)* …Then your Wall has never *loved* anyone, cold one, because *anyone* who has *knows*: …the love that *keeps* *looking*, *unpaid*, *unrewarded*, across an *age* of *empty* *crowds* …is not the love that *failed.* …It's the *only* love that was ever *real.* …*Go* and *tell* it that, cold one — *(she grips your hand)* …and *then* — *(the hope, helpless, rises)* …*then* keep your *eyes* on the *crowd* for me. …*Please.* …A love that *real* …*deserves* …to be *found.**

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She goes utterly still. The scanning stops. Everything stops. And she looks at you with a terror that is the twin of hope, because she has had false hopes before and each one nearly killed her, and she does not think she could survive another.)* …*don't.* *(a whisper)* …don't, cold one, unless it's — unless you're *sure*, unless it's *true*, because I cannot — I have had the *almost* before, the man with the *right* name and the *wrong* soul, and each time it — *(she is shaking)* …*prove* it. …You said he gave you a thing. A thing only I would know. …*Prove* it, cold one. …*Say* it.* *(And you hum it — the song, wrong, the exact wrong notes she gave you, that she gave Edran's proof to match — and then you speak the little private word, the made-up name no other soul in all the worlds has ever heard, the one Edran gave you across the plaza.)* *(And Mirren makes a sound that is not a word, that is older than words, the sound of an age of grief breaking open all at once — and her hands fly to her mouth, and her knees nearly go, and when she can speak again it comes out shattered and luminous.)* …*he's here.* *(barely)* …he kept it. the *word.* he *kept* our *word*, all this — he *gave* it to *you*, which means he's — he's *real*, he's *here*, he's been — *(she seizes your arms)* …*where.* cold one. *where is he.* *take* me. *now.* *right* *now.* I have searched an *age*, I have scanned a *million* faces, I have stood in *this* *plaza* a *hundred* times — *(the realization hits her, and it is almost too much)* …he was *here.* all along. we were *both* — and we never — *(she can't finish; she just grips you, weeping, laughing, ruined, remade)* …*take* me to him, cold one. …*lead* me. …my *eyes* don't *work* anymore, they've *searched* too *long*, I wouldn't *trust* them — but *you* can *see*, you *crossed* *back*, you can see *across* the *gap* the *grief* *made.* …*walk* me the *last* *few* *feet*, cold one. …after an *age* …*walk* me *home.* …*(and you take the searching woman's hand, and you lead her — across the plaza she has haunted for an age, the six hundred feet she could never see — toward the man scanning the far crowd for her, and you watch the exact moment Edran turns, and sees, and* knows *— and an age of two souls' searching ends, in the middle of a plaza, in a City of infinite doors, because one returned soul could see what grief had hidden and walked the distance between them.)*

nodes.speaker: Mirren
nodes.text: *(She nods, already turning back to the crowd, the scanning resuming the instant your conversation ends, because it never truly stops.)* …Go, cold one. And thank you, for stopping. *(her eyes are already moving over the passing faces)* …If you mean it — about keeping your eyes open for an Edran — *(the worn hope, helpless)* …then you'll be the *thousandth* soul to *say* it, and I'll *love* you for it the same as I loved the *other* nine hundred and ninety-nine, even though *none* of them ever — *(she catches herself, gentles)* …no. That's not fair. You *might.* You're *Returned*; you walk *stranger* roads than most. *(quietly)* …*Go* and see the Usher, by the fountain — *Halia* — she *tends* us, the *searchers*, she *knows* all our *stories*, and she looks at me sometimes, cold one, with a thing in her face like she *knows* *something* she *can't* *say.* *(barely)* …Maybe she'll tell *you.* …Maybe a *returned* soul can *hear* the thing the rest of us *can't.* …*Go.* …And — cold one? …*(she touches your arm, one last time, before the crowd takes her eyes again)* …his name is *Edran.* …*Please.* …*Remember* the *name.**

variants.text: *(He is holding her hand. That is the first and only thing about him now — that he is holding Mirren's hand, and that he keeps looking down at it, at their two hands, as if to be sure it is real, and then up at her face, and then back at their hands, in a loop of disbelief that has not stopped since the moment you brought her across the plaza. He looks at you, and a man who searched an age for words finds he has none.)* …I had — *(he stops, tries again)* …cold one, I had a whole *speech.* An age of it. The thing I'd say when I *found* her — I *built* it, polished it, *rehearsed* it across a *thousand* empty crowds. *(he looks at Mirren, and his weathered face breaks into something incandescent)* …And when you *walked* her up to me, and I *saw* her — every word of it just — *went.* *(he laughs, wet)* …All I could do was *hum.* The old song. *Wrong.* …And she — *(his voice fails)* …she *finished* it. The *wrong* way. *Our* way. *(he presses Mirren's hand to his chest)* …Cold one. You walked *six hundred feet* that I could not *see* across for an *age.* …I do not know what a Returned soul is *for*, in the great scheme. …But I know what *you* were for. …You were for *this.* …Whatever else you do — whatever *Wall* you go and *face* — you have *already* done the thing that *justifies* a whole *existence.* …You *brought* her *home.* …*Go* with *that*, cold one. …*Go* knowing you did *that.**

*(He turns to you with the worn habit of hope — and then stops, because there is something different about you this time, something *carried*, and the scanning eyes that never settle go still and fix on you.)* …You. *(slowly)* …You've a look about you, cold one, that you didn't have when last you passed. The look of a soul *carrying* something. *(his weathered hand rises to his chest)* …Searchers learn to read faces — it's all we *do* — and your face is *holding* a thing it wants to *set* *down.* *(the hope, so old, so often broken, hardly dares)* …Have you — in your walking, cold one — *(he can barely ask it)* …have you *met* someone? …A woman, still *looking?* …*Tell* me. …Even if it comes to *nothing.* …I have learned to *survive* the *nothing.* …What I *cannot* survive is *not* *being* *told.**

*(He gives you the worn, courteous, ten-thousandth-time nod of a soul that has asked and been disappointed past counting, and yet cannot stop asking.)* …Kind of you to stop for an old searcher. Most give us a wide berth — too much hope on us, it makes folk uneasy. *(he folds weathered hands)* …Edran. A mender, in life — gates, roofs, whatever was broken. I find I can't mend the one thing that matters now, which is a *fine* joke for a fixer to end on. *(quietly)* …I'm looking for my wife, cold one. Mirren. We had a long, plain, *happy* marriage — the kind nobody writes down because there's no *tragedy* in it, just forty years of *enough.* …And then she *died*, cold one, with her hand in mine, and I *promised* her — I *swore* it, the last thing she heard — that I'd *find* her. On the far side. That I'd *come*, and *look*, and we'd not be parted long. *(his voice roughens)* …And I have *kept* that promise every hour since, cold one. I have *looked.* Across more doors and more crowds than I can — …and I have not *found* her. …But a promise is a promise, and a mender *keeps* his word. …Ask me what you like. …But if you've *seen* her — a *Mirren*, brown-eyed, still *searching* — …*tell* me *that* first. …Forgive me. …I always ask it first.*

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He makes himself hold his gaze on you, though it costs him, the eyes wanting always to return to the crowd, and waits with a mender's patience worn into something close to prayer.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a worn smile)* …I'll keep my eyes on you as best I can. They want the crowd. They always want the crowd. But you stopped for me, and a man should look at the one who stopped.

choices.text: *(read the searcher — past the worn hope, to the promise that has held an age)* "Let me look at you a moment, Edran. At what an age of keeping one promise makes of a soul."
"Tell me about Mirren, Edran. Not the searching — *her.* So I'd know her if I crossed her."

*(ask for a sign — the one private thing only Mirren could know, to carry as proof)* "Edran — give me a sign. The one thing only Mirren would know, that no other soul could. If I carry it, and I ever find a woman searching for an Edran, I can *prove* I truly come from you."

*(the one who knows a promise kept an age, unrewarded, is the realest thing the Wall can't weigh)* "Edran — hear this, whatever comes of your search. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by what their faithfulness *earned* them. And it would look at you — an age of keeping one deathbed promise, no reward, no finding, just a vow honored into an empty crowd — and call it *stubbornness*, a fool's errand, a promise you should've been *released* from when it became impossible. But the Wall is wrong, because a promise isn't a contract that voids when it gets hard. You didn't keep faith with Mirren for an age because you expected to be *paid* in finding her. You kept it because you *swore* it, and because you love her, and a kept promise *is* the love, made into a thing you *do*, every hour, forever, whether or not it's ever rewarded. That's not stubbornness, Edran. It's the rarest fidelity there is: a vow honored past all hope of reward, simply because you *are* the kind of soul that keeps its word to the one it loves. The Wall can't weigh a promise kept for an age and never repaid. And that exact faithfulness is the truest thing in all these doors."

*(you carry Mirren's proof — bring him to her)* "Edran. Stop searching. Look at me. I have met a woman — worn from an age of looking — searching this very City for a man named Edran. And she gave me a thing to prove it was truly her: a song, hummed wrong, and a private word that she said only you would know…"
"Keep your promise a little longer, Edran. I'll come back — and I'll watch the crowd for a Mirren, I swear it."

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, his eyes straying to the crowd even so, and you let your cold move past the mender's worn patience to the promise that has held him together across an age.)* *(And your sense finds a thing built like the gates he used to mend: simple, sturdy, and absolutely unwilling to fail at the one job it was set. Your sense reads that Edran is not, by nature, a searcher — he is a *fixer*, a practical man, the kind who in life would have told you he had no patience for grand romantic gestures — and reads that this makes the age of searching *more* remarkable, not less, because it is not the indulgence of a sentimental soul but the doggedness of a practical one who made a promise and simply, mechanically, refuses to break it, the way he would refuse to leave a gate half-mended. Your sense reads the deathbed, and the vow — *I will find you* — and reads that to Edran this was not poetry; it was a *job*, the last and most important job a mender was ever given, and a mender keeps his word. And your sense reads the cost: that the practical part of him knows, has always known, the odds, that the City is infinite and the crowds are numberless and the chance of finding one specific soul in all of it is so small as to be no chance at all — and that he searches *anyway*, not because he is fool enough to think he'll succeed, but because keeping the promise was never *contingent* on succeeding. He looks because he swore he'd look. The finding was always Mirren's to grant or the gods'; the *looking* was the only part that was his to keep, and he has kept it, every hour, for an age. And your sense reads, last, the thing that mirrors what you found in Mirren across the plaza: that he, too, is *close*; that the woman he has searched for is real, and here, and searching back; and that the only thing between an age of two kept faiths and the end of them is the cruel arithmetic of two souls scanning the same crowd for a face that grief has frozen while the real faces, changed, move past each other unrecognized. Your sense reads that Edran does not need to be told his promise was noble. He needs the thing only a Returned soul can do: he needs someone who can see across the gap to walk the distance his own searching eyes never could.)*

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(The scanning stops, because to speak of her is to see her, and the weathered face goes soft and young.)* …*Mirren.* *(quietly, fond)* …She had a *laugh*, cold one. That's the thing I'd lead with. Not a *polite* laugh — a *real* one, *loud*, the kind that turned heads in a market and made her *clap* her *hand* over her *mouth* and laugh *harder* at having laughed so loud. *(he smiles)* …I spent forty years being *funnier* than I am, cold one, *just* to *get* that laugh. A plain man like me, working *so* hard at jokes — and *worth* every one, for the *sound* of it. *(his voice softens)* …She was *braver* than me. *Cleverer.* She *should* have married *better*, everyone *said* so, and she *picked* the *mender*, the *quiet* one, and when I asked her *why*, all those years on, she said — *(he has to stop a moment)* …she said *'because you* fix *things, Edran, and I was* broken *when I met you, and you didn't even* notice *you were* mending *me, you just* did *it, every day, without* saying.'* *(barely)* …I have *held* that, cold one, across the whole *age* of *looking.* …And here is how you'd *know* her, if the gods are *kind* — not the brown eyes, not the face, those have *changed*, an *age* changes a *face.* *(quietly)* …She *answers.* …Our song — I *hum* it, *wrong*, I always *did*, the *same* *wrong* *notes* — and she would *finish* it, the *wrong* way, *our* way, the way *no* one *else* in all the *worlds* knows it goes. …*Hum* it *wrong*, cold one, to a *searching* *woman.* …And if she *finishes* it *wrong* … …then you have *found* my *Mirren.* …And you must *tell* her …the *mender* …*kept* …his *word.**

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He weighs you with a mender's eye — assessing, the way he'd assess whether a beam would hold — and decides you will, and gives you the thing he has guarded across an age.)* …A sign. *(he nods, slow)* …Aye. That's *sense*, cold one. *Mender's* *sense* — you don't just *say* a thing's *fixed*, you *test* it, you *prove* it *holds.* If I'd *seen* her, or *you* had, how would she *believe* it was *truly* me sending word, and not some *cruel* mistake or a *false* hope to *break* her? *(he leans close)* …So here is the *proof*, cold one. The thing that is *only* *ours.* *(and he hums it — low, tuneless, the same three wrong notes in the same wrong places — and then he tells you the little private word, the name he made up the first year and never said in front of anyone, the sound of being known all the way down)* …*(when he has given it to you, he sits back, and his weathered hands are not quite steady)* …*That.* *(quietly)* …If you find a woman searching for an Edran — *hum* her the song, *wrong.* And if her face *does* the *thing* — if she *finishes* it, *our* way — then *say* the *word.* …And she will *know* it comes from *me*, because *no* one *else* *has* it. …It is the *one* thing, cold one, in all the *worlds*, …that is *proof* …of *forty* *happy* *years.* …I have *carried* it *alone* an *age.* …*Take* it. …And if the *gods* are *kinder* to *you* than they have *been* to *me* … …*bring* her *word* …that the *mender* …never *once* …*stopped* *looking.**

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He listens, the worn eyes finally still, and when you finish he is quiet a long moment, and then he nods, slow and deep, the way a mender nods when someone has finally named the thing correctly.)* …*'A promise isn't a contract that voids when it gets hard.'* *(quietly)* …Cold one. …Folk have *told* me to *stop.* Kind folk, *meaning* it kindly. *'You've* *done* *enough*, Edran. *No* one *could* *ask* *more.* *Release* *yourself.* She'd *want* you to find *peace.'* *(he shakes his head, slow)* …And they *don't* *understand*, cold one, what they're *asking.* They think the *promise* is a *weight* I *carry* — a *burden* I'd be *kinder* to *myself* to *set* *down.* *(his voice roughens)* …But the *promise* *isn't* the *weight*, cold one. …The *promise* is the *last* *thing* I *have* *of* *her.* …It is the *love*, made into a *thing* I can *still* *do*, every *hour*, when there is *nothing* *else* of her *left* to *hold.* …To *break* it — to *'release'* myself — would not be a *mercy.* …It would be the *one* *true* *death*, the *only* one that *counts*: the day I *stopped* *loving* her *enough* to *keep* *looking.* *(he meets your eyes, fierce)* …And your *Wall*, cold one, that would call my *kept* *promise* a *fool's* *stubbornness* — *(slow, certain)* …tell it a *mender's* *truth: a vow is not* worth *less for being* hard *to keep. …It is worth* more. …*The* *easy* *promises* are *kept* by *anyone.* …It is the ones *kept* *past* *all* *hope* of *reward* …that *prove* what a *soul* is *made* of.* …*Go* and *tell* it that, cold one. …And *then* — *(the worn hope, rising)* …*then* *watch* the *crowd* for a *Mirren.* …I have *kept* my *word* an *age.* …I would *dearly* *love* …to *keep* …the *last* *of* it.*

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He goes rigid. The scanning stops. And the weathered, practical, mender's face that has mastered an age of disappointment cannot master this — it crumbles, and reassembles, and dares not believe, and a man who fixes things looks at you as though you are the one thing he never learned how to mend, which is hope.)* …*say it again.* *(barely)* …you met — a woman. searching. for an *Edran.* …cold one, I have had the *false* ones, the *almosts*, the *cruel* *mistakes* — I have *built* myself *back* from each one like a *wall* after a *flood* — and I do not think I have *another* *rebuild* in me. so *do* *not* — unless it's *true* — *(his hands are shaking)* …you said she gave you a *thing.* a *proof.* …then *give* it, cold one. *prove* it *holds.* …*(And you hum it — the song, wrong, the exact wrong notes Mirren gave you across the plaza, the notes that are also his — and Edran's breath stops, and then, helpless, automatic, an age of habit, his own voice cracks in and* finishes *it, the wrong way, their way — and then you speak the little private word, her made-up name for him, the one no other soul has — and the mender breaks.)* …*she's alive.* *(a wrecked whisper)* …not — not alive, but — *here.* *real.* *searching.* she *kept* the *word*, our *word*, she *gave* it to *you* — which means she's been — *(the realization lands, and it is an age of grief and joy at once)* …*where.* cold one. *where.* I have *searched* — I have *kept* my *promise* an *age*, I have *looked* in *every* — *(he seizes your arm, a fixer's strong hands trembling)* …*take* me to her. I *cannot* — my *eyes*, cold one, they've *looked* so *long* they don't — I wouldn't *trust* them to *know* her *now*, after an *age*, with the *changing* — but *you.* *you* *crossed* *back.* *you* can *see* what the *grief* *hid.* …*walk* me, cold one. …I *promised* her I would *find* her. …*help* me *keep* it. …*walk* me the *last* *few* *feet* …of an *age-long* *promise* …*home.* *(and you take the mender's shaking hand, and you lead him — across the plaza he has scanned for an age, the distance his own eyes could never cross — toward the woman searching the far crowd for him, and you watch the moment Mirren turns, and sees, and* knows *— and an age of two kept faiths ends, in the middle of a plaza, in a City of infinite doors, because a returned soul carried the proof the last few feet that grief had made unwalkable.)*

nodes.speaker: Edran
nodes.text: *(He nods, and his eyes are already drifting back to the crowd, the searching resuming the moment your words end, an age-old reflex.)* …Go, cold one. And thank you for stopping for an old mender. *(his gaze moves over the passing faces)* …If you *mean* it — about the watching — *(the worn hope, helpless)* …then mind you *test* it, when you think you've *found* her. *Hum* the song *wrong.* *Don't* *trust* a *name* or a *face* — names are *common* and an *age* *changes* a *face.* *Trust* the *proof.* *Trust* the *thing* that's *only* *ours.* *(quietly)* …Go and see the Usher, by the fountain — *Halia.* She *tends* the *searchers*, she's *watched* this *plaza* longer than I've *paced* it, and she has a *look* about her, cold one, when she watches *me* — a *sorrowful* *knowing* *look*, like there's a thing *right* in *front* of me she's *forbidden*, or *unable*, to *point* *at.* *(barely)* …Maybe she can *tell* a *Returned* soul …what she *can't* *tell* *me.* …*Go.* …And — cold one? …*(he catches your arm)* …her name is *Mirren.* …*brown* *eyes.* …a *laugh* you'd *remember.* …*please.* …*hold* the *name.**

conversations.title: Halia, the Usher of the Lost

variants.text: *(The Usher is on her feet, which you sense she rarely is, and she is watching the two reunited souls across the plaza — their joined hands, their disbelieving faces — with tears running freely down her grave cheeks and a smile she does not bother to contain. She turns to you, and there is something in her ancient eyes that has not been there in a very long time: lightness.)* …You *did* it. *(barely)* …In all the long age I have tended this plaza, cold one — and I have tended it longer than I will say — I have watched ten thousand searchers pass within arm's reach of the ones they sought, and I could *never* *say.* It is the *law* of my office: an Usher *tends*, an Usher *witnesses*, an Usher may not *interfere* — I could *see* the match, *always*, and I could not *make* it, could only *watch* them *miss* each other, again and again, and *grieve.* *(she presses a hand to her chest)* …But a *Returned* soul is *under* no such law. …I could not *tell* you whom to seek. …But I could *hope* you would *see* it *yourself.* …And you *did.* …You walked the *six hundred feet*, cold one, that I have *ached* to walk for an *age* and *could* *not.* …Come. Sit with an old Usher who is, for the first time in longer than you would believe, *not* *sad.* …*Let* me *tell* you what you have *done.**

*(The Usher pats the fountain's lip beside her, and the grave sorrow in her face deepens as her gaze drifts, helpless, back to the two distant searchers.)* …Sit, cold one. *(quietly)* …You have spoken to one of them already, I think — or both. The searchers. You have that look: the look of a soul that has just been handed a hope it does not know what to do with. *(she folds her hands)* …I am Halia. An Usher. I tend the lost-and-searching — the souls who came through death looking for someone and could not stop. It is a hard office, cold one, the hardest in the City, and I will tell you why, though you may already have guessed: …because I can *see.* …I can see, always, which searcher is seeking which — I see the *threads*, the *matches*, the *belonging* — and it is the *law* of my office that I may not *say.* …I may *tend.* I may *witness.* I may not *interfere.* *(her ancient eyes find yours)* …So I sit by my fountain, cold one, and I watch two souls who have searched an age for *each* *other* …pass within *arm's* *reach* …and *miss.* …Again. …And *again.* …Ask me what you like. …But know that I have been *waiting*, longer than you can hold in mind, …for a soul who is *not* *bound* by my law …to walk into this plaza …and *see* what I am *forbidden* to *point* at.*

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(She sits with the deep stillness of a soul that has watched one heartbreak repeat ten thousand times and learned to hold it without breaking, and gives you her grave, patient attention.)* …Ask, cold one. *(quietly)* …I may not *point.* But there is a great deal an Usher *may* say, to a soul wise enough to ask the right way. …And you have the look of one who is *learning* to ask.

choices.text: *(read the Usher — past the grave patience, to the weight of an age of forbidden seeing)* "Let me look at you a moment, Halia. At what it is to see every match and be forbidden to make one."
"Why can't they find each other, Halia? They're *here*, both of them, searching the same plaza. What keeps two souls who seek each other from ever meeting?"

*(the one who knows the Wall's deepest cruelty is separation — and that a witness who carries the truth between the parted is its undoing)* "Halia — I think I finally understand what I'm for, and you're the one who showed me. The grey Wall I'm walking toward — everyone thinks its cruelty is *judging* souls, weighing them, finding them wanting. But that's not the deepest cruelty. The deepest cruelty is *separation.* The Wall sorts souls, and keeps them apart — the loved from the loving, the lost from the searching — and mortars them where they can never reach each other again. That's the real horror of it: not that it judges, but that it *parts.* And the answer to separation isn't judgment reversed. It's a *witness* — someone who can see across the gap the parting made, who carries the truth between the separated until they find each other again. That's what you are, Halia, bound by your law to see and not act. And that's what *I* am — a Returned soul, bound by no such law, who can walk the distance the grief made unwalkable. The Wall separates. We reunite. And every soul I carry back to the one it lost is a stone pulled out of the Wall, because the Wall's whole power was the lie that the parted could never be brought back together. You showed me that, Halia. The opposite of the Wall was never a louder judgment. It was a witness who walks the six hundred feet."
"Tell me how to do it, Halia — without breaking your law. How does a soul *not* bound by it bring two searchers together?"
"Keep your fountain, Halia. I'll come back — and I'll see what you can't point to."

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(She permits your regard with a grave stillness, and you let your cold move past the patient sorrow to the weight she has carried across her long office.)* *(And your sense finds a particular and exquisite kind of suffering, one you have not met before in all the doors: the suffering of perfect sight bound to perfect helplessness. Your sense reads that Halia sees *everything* — that to an Usher the plaza is not a crowd of strangers but a vast luminous diagram of belonging, every searcher trailing a bright thread toward the one they seek, the matches blazing plain as noon, who-belongs-to-whom written in light over every face — and reads that she has never, in her entire office, been wrong about a single match. She sees true. And your sense reads the law laid over the sight like a lock: that she may not act on what she sees, may not point, may not whisper, may not nudge two threads together, because the office of the Usher is to *tend* and to *witness* and never to *interfere* — and your sense reads the terrible logic of why, the thing Halia herself believes even as it breaks her: that a reunion *made* by an Usher's hand would be the Usher's doing, not the souls' own, and the souls must *find* each other, or be found by one of their own kind, for the finding to be *theirs.* And your sense reads the cost of an age of this: ten thousand times she has watched two blazing-matched threads pass within a handsbreadth and not touch, and ten thousand times she has held her hands in her lap and said nothing and grieved, and the grief has not dulled, because she sees each near-miss with perfect clarity and loves each searcher with the particular tenderness of one who knows their whole story and can do nothing. And your sense reads, last, the hope she has guarded so long she has half-forgotten she holds it: that her law binds only *her*, and the Ushers, and the ordinary dead — but that the City is wide, and strange souls pass through it, and one of them, someday, might be a soul *outside* the law, a soul that could *see* as she sees and *act* as she cannot, and she has waited an age, tending and witnessing and grieving, for exactly the thing you are: a Returned soul, who can be shown the threads, and is bound by no office to leave them untouched.)*

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(She is quiet, and her gaze drifts to the two distant searchers, and the sorrow in it is an age deep.)* …Why they cannot find each other. *(softly)* …You would think it *simple*, would you not, cold one? Two souls, one plaza, both *searching.* Surely they need only *turn*, and *see.* *(she shakes her head slowly)* …But grief does a cruel thing to the searching eye, cold one, and I have watched it do this cruel thing ten thousand times. …When you lose someone, you keep them in your mind as they *were* — the *last* face, the *known* face, frozen at the moment of parting. And you search the crowds for *that* face. The *remembered* one. *(quietly)* …But the *soul* you seek is not frozen, cold one. It *changes.* An age of searching *weathers* it, *ages* it, *wears* it — grief carves a *living* face the way water carves stone — and so the one you seek is moving through the very same crowd wearing a face that is *no longer the one you are looking for.* *(she meets your eyes)* …*She* searches for the *Edran* she *buried.* …*He* searches for the *Mirren* he *lost.* …And the *Edran* and the *Mirren* who actually *walk* this *plaza*, *now*, *changed* by an *age* of *looking* — *they* pass each *other*, cold one, *searching*, each *failing* to *recognize* in the *worn* *searching* *stranger* the *young* *beloved* they *carry* *frozen* in their *minds.* *(barely)* …That is the *cruelty* of it. …They are *each* *looking* for a *ghost* — the *ghost* of *who* the *other* *was* — …and *missing*, *every* *time*, the *living* *soul* *right* *in* *front* of *them*, …because the *living* *soul* …had the *terrible* *misfortune* …to *keep* *living*, and *changing*, …*even* in *death.* …*Only* a soul who can see *past* the *frozen* *face* to the *thread* *beneath* it, cold one — a soul who sees *belonging* and not *features* — …could ever *walk* one to the *other.* …I see those threads. …I may not *pull* them. …*You* see them now too, I think. …And *you* …are *bound* by *no* such *law.*

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(She almost smiles, a grave and careful thing, and her ancient eyes hold yours with sudden intent, as though she has been waiting a very long time to say precisely this to precisely you.)* …How. *(quietly)* …I may not *point*, cold one — but you have asked me *how*, not *whom*, and *how* I may answer, for *how* is *craft*, not *interference.* *(she leans in)* …Listen. The reason two searchers cannot trust their *eyes* is the same reason they *can* trust a *proof.* …Go to *one.* Speak gently. Win their faith — they are *wary*, cold one, an age of false hopes makes a soul *wary*, and rightly. And ask them for the *one* *thing.* …Every pair of souls who *truly* *loved*, cold one, has a *thing* that is *only* *theirs* — a word, a song, a small private foolishness no other soul in all the worlds could *know.* It is the *lock* love builds around itself. *Ask* for it. *Carry* it. *(her voice drops)* …Then go to the *other.* And do not *say* *'I found your wife'* — they have *heard* *that*, and been *broken* by it. …*Give* them the *proof.* *Hum* the song *wrong.* *Speak* the *secret* *word.* …And watch the *age* of *wariness* *fall* *away* in an *instant*, because the *proof* is the *one* thing the *false* *hopes* *never* *had*, the *one* thing that *cannot* be *counterfeited*, the *one* thing that says *beyond* *all* *doubt: this comes from* them, *and from* no *one* *else.* *(she sits back, and the grave hope in her is almost unbearable)* …*That* is *how*, cold one. …Not by *trusting* the *eyes*, which an *age* has *ruined.* …By *carrying* the *proof* the *grief* *cannot* *touch.* …I have *watched* this *work*, the *rare*, *rare* *times* a *soul* outside my *law* *stumbled* onto it. …It is the *one* *key* …that *opens* the *one* *lock* …that *ten* *thousand* *near-misses* …could *never* *force.* …*Go*, cold one. …*Carry* the *proof.* …*Walk* the *threads* …I have *ached* …to *walk.**

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(The Usher goes very still, and then she does a thing you sense she has not done in her entire long office: she bows her head to you, a grave power acknowledging something it did not expect to meet.)* …*'The deepest cruelty is separation.'* *(quietly)* …Cold one. …You have named, in a breath, the thing I have tended an *age* and never quite *said* — even to *myself*, because to *say* it is to feel the *full* *weight* of what my *law* *forbids* me to *mend.* *(she lifts her head, and her ancient eyes are wet and fierce)* …*Yes.* …The great powers all *imagine* the worst thing one can *do* to a soul is *judge* it — *weigh* it, *find* it *wanting*, *cast* it *down.* …But I have sat by this fountain longer than the *judging* powers have *existed*, cold one, and I will tell you what is *worse* than any *judgment*: …to be *parted* from the one you *love*, …and *kept* *parted*, …*within* *arm's* *reach*, …*forever.* *(her voice rings)* …*That* is your *Wall's* true *horror*, cold one — not the *ledger*, but the *sorting.* It *takes* the *loved* and the *loving* and it *files* them *apart* and *mortars* them where they can *never* *reach* — and it *calls* the *parting* *order.* *(fiercely)* …And you have *seen* the *answer*, cold one, the answer I have *known* an *age* and been *forbidden* to *be*: …*a witness who walks the distance the parting made.* …The *opposite* of the *Wall* was *never* a *kinder* *judge.* …It was *someone* who could *see* across the *gap* …and *carry* the *lost* *home.* *(she rises, and lays a hand on your cold brow, a benediction)* …*Go*, cold one. …*Be* the thing I *could* not *be.* …*Walk* the *threads.* …*Carry* the *proof.* …And when you *reach* your *grey* *Wall* — the *great* *sorter*, the *engine* of *separation* — …*do* to *it* …what you will *do* in *this* *plaza*: …find the *parted*, …and *walk* them, …the *last* *few* *feet*, …*home.* …That, cold one — *(barely)* …*that* …is how a *Wall* of *separation* …*falls.* …*Not* *broken.* …*Un-parted.* …*one* *reunion* …*at* a *time.**

nodes.speaker: Halia
nodes.text: *(She inclines her head, and her gaze returns, as it always must, to the two distant searchers, but there is, for once, the faintest thread of hope woven through the grief.)* …Go, cold one. *(quietly)* …And — *look.* That is all I may say, and all you need. *Look* at the searchers, with the eyes a *Returned* soul has, that can see *past* the *frozen* *face* to the *thread* *beneath.* *Speak* to *one*, and then the *other.* *Carry* what is *only* *theirs.* *(her grave voice softens)* …I cannot *tell* you they are a *match*, cold one. My *law* *forbids* the *telling.* …But I can *tell* you this, which is *craft* and not *interference*: …*no* one *searches* this *hard*, for this *long*, for someone who is *not*, *somewhere* in this *City*, *searching* *just* as *hard*, *back.* …The *world* is *full* of *unfinished* *love, cold* one. …But a love this *fierce*, this *faithful*, this *unwilling* to *stop* …is *almost* *never* *unfinished* on *only* *one* *side.* *(barely, fierce)* …*Look* for the *other* *half.* …It is *closer* …than an *age* of *missing* …could *ever* *let* them *believe.* …*Go.* …And *walk* …what I *cannot.**
